original_txt:
Nature can help restore us as we deal with coronavirusAs a society we are experiencing a period of unprecedented stress and uncertainty But nature can be a guide to us at this time providing a model of cooperation a place to heal and ways to connect even at the recommended distance

Opinion

In nature cooperation abounds

As author Peter Wohlleben of The Hidden Life of Trees explored trees send distress signals about drought and disease for example or insect attacks and other trees alter their behavior when they receive these messages While social distancing feels like a sacrifice now the stay at home order has saved countless lives from highrisk populations to medical and service professionals Altering our behavior at this critical time just as nature does in the face of challenges is community resilience in action

Even at a safe distance nature heals

One study after another documents its positive effects on physical and mental health for people of all ages and backgrounds Nature offers balm and solace to the weary  being a source of beauty wonder and inspiration And nature can be found everywhere  including in the trees outside your apartment or home

Nature connects us

Looking out your window you can see it as the birds have begun their grand migration through our region Living in a major flyway residents can experience this annual spectacle just as those thousands of miles away experienced it reminding us just how interconnected we are

As we collectively wade through this worldwide pandemic natures resilience should serve as our guide Through collective cooperation connection and healing we will overcome this moment and thrive as nature blooms today

It is only right that we take this time to reconnect with it

Jerry Adelmann is the president  CEO of Openlands a conservation organization in the Chicago area
corr_txt:
As society grapples with coronavirus, Nature's wisdom stands as our beacon through these tumultuous times. It exemplifies cooperation—much like trees that communicate during drought or disease to support each other and adapt collectively (as explored by author Peter Wohlleben in The Hidden Life of Trees). Social distancing now mirrors the self-preservation instinct found within nature, as it has already saved countless lives. This temporary adjustment is a testament to our ability for community resilience when faced with adversity—a lesson from Nature's own response to challenges (Opinion by Jerry Adelmann).

Even while adhering to recommended distances in nature offers solace, healing and connection. Studies consistently show that engagement with the natural world has tangible benefits for both physical health and mental well-being across various demographics—a serene escape amidst chaos (Nature's Balm).

The grandeur of bird migration serves as a powerful reminder of our interconnectedness, even when physically apart. Witnesses in major flyways can observe this annual spectacle that spans great distances and connects us to the broader world—an enduring symbol of unity (Nature's Connector).

As we navigate through a global pandemic together, Nature provides an example for resilience: it is within our power to overcome adversity. Through cooperation, connection, and healing inspired by nature itself that the future holds promise—a time when blooming life prevails (Nature's Guide). In this shared experience of crisis and recovery lies a unique opportunity for us all to reconnect with Nature in profound ways during these times apart from it.

It is only fitting, then, as we endure through the pandemic, that society takes time to rediscover its bond with nature (Reconnection Call). Jerry Adelmann's leadership at Openlands underscores our collective responsibility towards conservation and sustainability—a commitment echoed by Nature itself.
